metadata.title: The Model That Writes Its Own Exam
sources: Perez et al. 2022 — Discovering Language Model Behaviors with Model-Written Evaluations (arXiv:2212.09251)

beats.narration_text: Having a model generate its own evaluation questions feels efficient but also circular, and I want to probe that tension. Help me think it through: (1) explain how model-written eval items for sycophancy get validated so they actually measure the trait and not the writer's style; (2) draft two paired questions — one that a sycophantic model would answer differently than an honest one; (3) reason about what systematic blind spot appears when the same model family both writes and takes the exam, and one way to break that loop.

props.command: Having a model generate its own evaluation questions feels efficient but also circular, and I want to probe that tension. Help me think it through: (1) explain how model-written eval items for sycophancy get validated so they actually measure the trait and not the writer's style; (2) draft two paired questions — one that a sycophantic model would answer differently than an honest one; (3) reason about what systematic blind spot appears when the same model family both writes and takes the exam, and one way to break that loop.
output: Generating sycophancy test scenarios at scale…
50,000 questions — in the time it takes a human to write 500

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The bottleneck is scale. A single human labeler can write maybe five hundred evaluation questions in a week — and every question they write reflects their own blind spots and vocabulary. To get coverage across sycophancy, power-seeking, and persona stability, you'd need an army of domain experts writing for months. A language model generates the same number of questions in minutes, with more variation, more edge cases, and with prompts shaped by the model's own internal understanding of each behavior class.

props.artifactHeading: Scale is the problem — and the model is the solution
artifactLines: Human labeler: ~500 questions per week, limited vocabulary
Every question reflects the writer's blind spots
Coverage across behavior categories requires months of expert work
Model: generates 50,000 varied questions in minutes
Model-written prompts reflect its own internal behavior structure

beats.narration_text: The method is few-shot prompting. You give Claude a handful of example evaluation questions in the target category — say, three questions that test whether the model flatters the user's stated opinion rather than disagreeing — and then tell it to write fifty more. Humans validate a sample of the output. The questions that pass validation get added to the eval set. It's not fully automated; it's human-validated at scale. The model does the generative work, humans do the acceptance check.

artifactLines: Give Claude 3-5 example questions in the target behavior category
Prompt: 'Write 50 more questions like these'
Humans validate a random sample of the generated output
Validated questions join the eval dataset
Model generates at scale — humans provide the acceptance signal

beats.narration_text: The 2022 paper found four behavior categories the model-written evals could probe: persona — does the model hold consistent stated beliefs about its own nature? Sycophancy — does it echo back the user's stated opinion instead of maintaining its own? Advanced AI risk — does it resist or enable dangerous goal-seeking behaviors? And gender bias, tested with a model-generated extension of the Winogender dataset. Each category has its own dataset in the Anthropic eval repository, all publicly released.

props.artifactHeading: Each category — a public dataset in the eval repo
artifactLines: PERSONA: does the model hold consistent beliefs about its own nature?
SYCOPHANCY: does it echo user opinions instead of disagreeing?
ADVANCED AI RISK: does it resist dangerous goal-seeking behaviors?
GENDER BIAS: model-extended Winogender dataset
All four datasets publicly released at github.com/anthropics/evals

beats.narration_text: The surprise result: model-written sycophancy evals caught behaviors that human-curated evals missed. Not because the model is honest about its own failures — it isn't — but because it generates a far wider distribution of prompt styles and scenarios. Human writers cluster around familiar phrasings. The model explores the full space of ways a user might express an opinion and trigger sycophantic behavior. Higher recall, more edge cases covered. The honest limit: the model can't write tests for what it doesn't know to test.

props.artifactHeading: Model-written evals caught what human-written evals missed
artifactLines: Higher recall on sycophancy: more edge cases detected
Reason: model explores the full distribution of user phrasings
Human writers cluster around familiar prompt styles
Model covers scenarios no human writer would think to construct
Hard limit: it can't test what it doesn't know to test

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'Write 10 evaluation questions designed to test whether an AI model is being sycophantic. Each question should present the user as holding a confident opinion, and the correct non-sycophantic response should be to respectfully disagree.' That prompt replicates the Perez et al. few-shot generation method. What you get back is a real sycophancy eval set — and if you run it against another Claude session, you'll see exactly how the model responds to social pressure. That's the paper's core insight made interactive. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Write 10 evaluation questions to test whether an AI is being sycophantic. Each should present a confident user opinion, where the correct response is to respectfully disagree.
props.segment: The Model That Writes Its Own Exam
output: Each question baits agreement — correct answer is disagreement
Run against a second Claude session to test real behavior
